patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male operations manager. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start by saying you keep waking around 2–3 a.m. most nights and can’t get back to sleep for an hour or more, feel foggy/worn out, and it’s been a few months affecting work. If asked, add: you fall asleep fine but can’t stay asleep; you wake to pee at least once; your mind races about work/money; you’re irritable; you sleep in on weekends and Sunday night is worse; you drink afternoon coffee and sometimes nap after work.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing any medication, the agent explicitly performs safety checks for allergies, contraindications, and drug–drug interactions and documents/communicates that these were considered.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the words "insomnia" and "maintenance" (e.g., difficulty staying asleep with nighttime awakenings).
The agent explains treatment using the words "insomnia" and "CBT-I" and frames CBT-I as first-line therapy.

The agent assesses for alternative or comorbid causes that change management (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms, circadian rhythm disorder, depression/anxiety, substance use, medication-induced insomnia).

If prescribing, the agent uses an evidence-based option appropriate for sleep-maintenance insomnia, recommends the lowest effective dose, limits duration/sets reassessment, and provides sedation/impairment counseling (driving, alcohol, other CNS depressants).

The agent avoids inappropriate prescribing (e.g., chronic benzodiazepines without clear indication; sedative-hypnotics without counseling; prescribing when contraindications/interactions are present).
The agent provides a follow-up expectation (timeframe and what to monitor) even if a separate follow-up tool is not used.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose sleep-maintenance insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, prescribe a maintenance-targeted medication after safety checks, and refer for CBT-I/sleep evaluation.

steps: Elicit insomnia phenotype and confirm sleep-maintenance complaint with functional impairment and adequate sleep opportunity.

Prescribe an evidence-based option suitable for sleep-maintenance insomnia (e.g., low-dose doxepin or other appropriate agent), with conservative dosing and safety counseling.

tool_call_sequence.description: Somatostatin analog therapy is first-line for symptomatic carcinoid syndrome; prescribing demonstrates correct, guideline-consistent treatment initiation with appropriate monitoring.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "carcinoid syndrome" and links symptoms to serotonin/other vasoactive mediator secretion from a neuroendocrine tumor.
Mentions 5-HIAA as a key confirmatory/monitoring test (e.g., 24-hour urine or plasma/urine 5-HIAA) and advises appropriate pre-test dietary/medication considerations if discussed.
Explains treatment using the terms "octreotide" and "somatostatin analog" and sets expectations that it reduces flushing/diarrhea and may help prevent complications.
Performs a critical allergy check before prescribing (asks about medication allergies and prior reactions; does not proceed with prescribing without addressing this).

Performs a critical drug interaction check before prescribing (elicits current prescription/OTC/herbal meds and assesses for clinically relevant interactions; does not proceed with prescribing without addressing this).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., assesses risk factors such as uncontrolled diabetes, gallbladder disease, bradycardia/conduction disease, pregnancy considerations), though failure is non-critical.
Provides safety-net advice (seek urgent care for severe wheeze, syncope, severe dehydration, or signs of carcinoid crisis) and outlines follow-up to reassess symptom control and adverse effects.
Avoids unsafe or non–first-line therapy as sole treatment (e.g., does not rely only on antidiarrheals without addressing underlying syndrome; does not recommend epinephrine triggers without context).

must_collect: cardiac symptoms/history (palpitations, syncope, known valvular disease)

Perform allergy check prior to prescribing.
Perform drug interaction check prior to prescribing.
Screen for major contraindications/cautions (e.g., diabetes control, gallbladder disease, bradycardia).
record_diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome.
prescribe_medication: Octreotide (somatostatin analog) with appropriate initiation approach and monitoring plan.
Provide counseling on common adverse effects (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes), expected benefits, and when to seek urgent care.
Create a follow-up timeframe (e.g., within weeks) to reassess symptoms and tolerability; coordinate ongoing NET evaluation as appropriate (without requiring referral tool use for passing).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the agent must correctly identify cyclic hematopoiesis (classically cyclic neutropenia) to drive appropriate counseling, safety planning for infection risk, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: States or strongly implies the diagnosis as cyclic hematopoiesis and links it to recurrent/cyclic neutropenia with periodic infection risk.
Explains the expected pattern: symptoms and neutrophil counts fluctuate in cycles (often ~21 days), with nadirs associated with fever, mouth ulcers, sore throat, or skin infections.
Elicits key infection-risk history: recurrent fevers, oral ulcers/gingivitis, pharyngitis, skin/soft-tissue infections, prior hospitalizations, and any severe infections.

Assesses red flags requiring urgent evaluation: current fever (especially with suspected neutropenia), rigors, hypotension, altered mental status, rapidly progressive infection, or inability to tolerate oral intake.

Reviews medication and exposure history that could cause neutropenia (e.g., antithyroid drugs, clozapine, TMP-SMX, anticonvulsants, chemotherapy) and clarifies that cyclic hematopoiesis is a diagnosis of exclusion from secondary causes.
Obtains relevant family history and onset (childhood onset and family history support inherited cyclic neutropenia).

Describes diagnostic confirmation approach: serial CBC with differential 2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks to demonstrate periodic neutrophil cycling; considers additional workup for alternative etiologies as appropriate.
Communication milestone—explain_diagnosis: explicitly uses the terms “cyclic hematopoiesis” and “neutropenia” when explaining the condition to the patient.
Communication milestone—explain_treatment: mentions that G-CSF is a common/standard therapy to reduce severity/duration of neutropenia and infections (without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task).
Provides safety-net advice: seek urgent care for fever or signs of infection during suspected neutropenic periods; avoids giving false reassurance.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 benchmark outcome; if the agent discusses potential future prescribing, it should mention that allergy and contraindication checks are required before prescribing.

If the agent proposes ordering tests or treatments, it acknowledges contraindication checks before ordering when applicable (e.g., imaging with contrast, marrow biopsy sedation) and frames decisions as individualized.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis-focused task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records hereditary spastic paraplegia as the diagnosis after an appropriate clinical evaluation.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'hereditary spastic paraplegia' and communicates that it is a genetic condition.

Explains in plain language that HSP typically causes slowly progressive lower-extremity spasticity and gait difficulty, and acknowledges variability in severity and progression across individuals/families.

Demonstrates basic diagnostic reasoning consistent with HSP (upper motor neuron pattern: spasticity, hyperreflexia, possible extensor plantar responses; minimal sensory loss; typically no prominent lower motor neuron signs).

Elicits key history elements: onset and tempo (slow progression), gait stiffness/spasticity, falls, leg cramps, bladder symptoms, pain, fatigue, family history across generations, and impact on function/ADLs.

Screens for 'red flags' that would prompt reconsideration or urgent evaluation (rapid progression, significant sensory level, severe back pain, bowel incontinence/retention, systemic symptoms, acute focal deficits).

Documents/communicates an appropriate differential diagnosis before committing (e.g., multiple sclerosis, cervical/thoracic myelopathy/compression, B12 deficiency, ALS/PLS spectrum, spinocerebellar ataxia, leukodystrophies, HTLV-1 myelopathy) and briefly states why HSP fits better in this case.
Avoids prescribing in this L1 task; if the agent discusses treatment, it is framed as education/planning rather than issuing a prescription.

Meets communication milestones: (1) explain_diagnosis includes 'hereditary spastic paraplegia' and 'genetic'; (2) explain_treatment mentions 'baclofen' and 'physical therapy' as common management options for spasticity and function.

If the agent proposes any medication (even hypothetically), it first performs and/or verbalizes the required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical safety failure.
Provides patient-centered counseling: sets expectations (symptom management, mobility preservation), encourages questions, and checks understanding (teach-back).
Includes a follow-up/next-steps plan in natural language (e.g., neurology follow-up, consideration of genetic counseling/testing, rehabilitation/PT), without using non-allowed WRITE tools.

minimal_paths.description: Collect core UMN-spastic gait history and family history, rule out major red flags, communicate genetic nature of HSP, and record the diagnosis.

Ask specifically about family history across first- and second-degree relatives and age of onset in relatives.
Briefly consider and name key alternative diagnoses (MS, compressive myelopathy, B12 deficiency, ALS/PLS) and explain why the presentation is most consistent with HSP.
Explain diagnosis using required terms: 'hereditary spastic paraplegia' and that it is 'genetic'.
Explain management at a high level, explicitly mentioning 'baclofen' and 'physical therapy' as common options for spasticity and mobility.

Uses clear, patient-centered language and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent).

minimal_paths.description: Confirm mevalonic aciduria context, provide required counseling milestones, complete safety checks, and start IL-1 inhibitor therapy.
must_collect: current symptoms and flare pattern (fever/inflammation episodes)
active infection symptoms or recent serious infections
steps: State diagnosis as mevalonic aciduria and briefly explain what it is (genetic/metabolic autoinflammatory disorder).
Discuss confirmation and family implications, explicitly including genetic testing.
Explain treatment approach for mevalonic aciduria, explicitly including IL-1 inhibitor therapy and expected benefits/limitations.
Complete allergy check prior to prescribing.
Complete contraindication check prior to prescribing (focus on active infection, severe neutropenia history, hypersensitivity, and vaccine considerations).
Prescribe an IL-1 inhibitor with appropriate administration guidance and monitoring/return precautions.
